text: This is a video of a robotic left upper lobectomy performed at the University of Michigan. Our patient is a seventy-three-year-old woman found to have a twelve millimeter left upper lobe nodule on a screening CT scan. A PET scan was obtained with mild hypermetabolic activity. She was otherwise asymptomatic. Her past medical history included hypertension and COPD. Her pulmonary function tests were adequate. Decision was made to take her to the operating room for a left upper lobe wedge resection. followed by a lobectomy pending the results of the frozen section.

The patient is positioned in the right lateral decubitus position, and four robotic ports are placed in the eighth intercostal space, including one in the anterior axillary line. The diaphragm is marked on the skin for potential assistant port placement.

The surgeon performs a wedge resection using a robotic green load stapler, excising the nodule adjacent to the fissure with gross negative margins. Following this, the mediastinal lymph node dissection begins, with the surgeon using fenestrated bipolar forceps in arm one and a curved bipolar dissector in arm three to take down the inferior pulmonary ligament. The dissection progresses up the anterior pleura until the inferior pulmonary vein is identified and freed.
The surgeon opens the posterior pleura to access the site where level seven lymph nodes are located, revealing the underlying anatomical structures.

The surgeon uses caudate forceps to hold lymph nodes and resects them with bipolar forceps. The pleura adjacent to the aorta above the pulmonary artery is opened, revealing lymph nodes, the phrenic nerve, pulmonary artery, and aorta. Gauze is inserted to keep the field clear before dissecting around the lingular branch of the superior pulmonary vein and two additional branches to the left upper lobe.

The surgeon uses the white load of the robotic stapler to divide the most apical branch of the superior pulmonary vein, revealing the anterior branches of the pulmonary artery behind it. The dissection continues around these branches, and they are divided with the stapler. The main pulmonary artery is visible diving down behind the freed branches, allowing for safe division of both branches with a single stapler load.

The surgeon divides the remaining branches of the superior pulmonary vein and exposes the bronchus of the left upper lobe. Using gauze and robotic suction, the field is kept clear for blunt dissection behind the bronchus, avoiding injury to the pulmonary artery branches. The bronchus is clamped and divided with the green load of the robotic stapler. After transection, the remaining branches of the pulmonary artery to the left upper lobe are individually dissected and divided.

The surgeon uses a curved tip white load robotic stapler to safely transect the second branch of the pulmonary artery. The remaining fissure is completed with multiple loads of the green load robotic stapler, ensuring the staple line remains above the stapler and the transected vessels are positioned below. The specimen of the left upper lobe is placed into a large specimen bag and extracted through the anterior port site, which is enlarged to approximately three to four centimeters for removal.

And then also not shown here, we completed an intercostal nerve block for post-operative pain control and we placed a twenty-eight French chest tube through the inferior port. Her final pathology was a T-one-a N-zero tumor Zero out of eight lymph nodes positive. Thank you very much.
